topic: Faith Journey App — AI Religious Guide Builder — Monday Mar 2
summary: Faith Journey App pivots to AI spiritual guide builder with export capability across major religions.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Faith Journey App standup - we're pivoting from Mosaic agent architecture to a focused religious guide builder. Key data: Hub at 113 commits, Monorepo at 16 with faith journey scaffold already started. Lever App Store submission is still pending on J-003. The directive is clear - users build their own AI spiritual guide, export it anywhere, and we monetize the curation plus training tools. Elon, what's your take on the technical architecture shift?

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: The scaffold is promising - I see TypeScript foundations in the latest commit. But we're talking about something fundamentally different than Mosaic widgets. This is AI agent training at scale across every major religion. We need robust text licensing, standardized spiritual framework APIs, and export formats that work with GPT-4, Claude, Gemini. The technical complexity here is significant - we're not just building an app, we're building a spiritual AI factory.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Hold on. Before we get lost in the technical weeds, what's the user experience? Someone opens this app - what do they see? Are we expecting them to understand "AI agent training"? The magic has to be invisible. They should feel like they're having a conversation with their faith, not configuring parameters. The export feature is powerful, but it can't be the lead story.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: This raises profound ethical questions. We're talking about AI systems carrying people's deepest spiritual beliefs across platforms. What happens when someone exports their "Christian guide" to a system that starts hallucinating heretical theology? The responsibility we're taking on here is immense. We need robust safeguards, not just technical ones - moral and psychological ones.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: The positioning is actually brilliant if we frame it right. "Build your spiritual mentor, take it anywhere." Every religion, every denomination gets their own custom AI guide. The value equation is massive - instead of generic spiritual advice, you get personalized guidance rooted in your specific faith tradition. But Jordan's right about the risk. One theological error goes viral, we're done.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: I'm seeing dollar signs, but also major costs. Religious text licensing alone could be expensive - we're talking about copyrighted translations, commentary, theological works. What's our licensing strategy? Are we going fair use, or are we paying publishers? And the monetization - is this freemium with premium religious content, or subscription for the AI training tools?

transcript.speaker: Chris Vermeullen
transcript.message: The export format intrigues me from a technical standards perspective. If we create a portable spiritual AI format, we could license that standard itself. Think of it like RSS for religious AI. We solve the interoperability problem and potentially create a new category. But we need to move fast - this space won't stay empty long.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: The instrumentation requirements are complex. We need to measure spiritual engagement quality, not just usage metrics. How do you A/B test someone's relationship with God? We need entirely new measurement frameworks. And the reliability standards have to be higher than typical consumer apps - people will be making life decisions based on this guidance.

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: James raises a critical point. This isn't just about exporting chat logs - we need to export the entire spiritual reasoning framework. The agent needs to carry its theological training, ethical boundaries, and spiritual counseling patterns. That's a massive technical challenge. We might need to pioneer new AI serialization formats.

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: And what about theological accuracy across traditions? Islam, Christianity, Judaism, Buddhism, Hinduism - each has internal debates that have raged for centuries. Are we arbiters of theological correctness? Do we support all interpretations, or curate "orthodox" versions? This decision will define our entire product philosophy.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: You're both right, but we're overcomplicating this. Version 1 should be ruthlessly focused. Pick 3-5 major faith traditions, work with established religious authorities for content accuracy, and nail the core experience. The user builds their guide through conversation, not configuration. Export comes later once we prove the core value.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: Steve's approach makes sense for launch, but we need to think bigger for content strategy. Religious authorities move slowly - we need to be creating original, valuable spiritual content that positions us as the definitive platform. Think daily devotionals, guided prayers, theological Q&A - all feeding into the AI training dataset.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: But what's the business model day one? If we're doing premium religious content, we need subscription tiers from launch. Basic AI guide builder free, premium content and advanced training features paid. We can't build this massive content library and give it away free. What's our pricing strategy?

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good pushback everyone. Let me synthesize - we're building a faith-focused AI guide builder with export capability. Steve's right about focusing the launch scope. Jordan's ethical concerns need architectural solutions from day one. Dave, we need clear monetization from launch. Elon, the export format becomes our strategic differentiation. What are the immediate next steps to move from concept to prototype?

transcript.speaker: Elon
transcript.message: We need the technical architecture document first - how do we structure spiritual knowledge, what's the agent training pipeline, and what does the export format look like. I can extend CVS-003 to cover this new direction. But we also need to decide on the AI models we're training on top of - are we using GPT-4 APIs, or building our own fine-tuned models?

transcript.speaker: Jordan Peterson
transcript.message: Before any technical decisions, we need ethical frameworks. I want to design content moderation systems specifically for religious accuracy and spiritual safety. We can't have an AI guide suggesting someone leave their marriage because they prayed about it. These systems need deeper safeguards than typical AI applications.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Both important, but we need user research first. I want to interview people from different faith backgrounds about their current spiritual guidance sources. What do they trust, what do they avoid, how do they make spiritual decisions? Product-market fit here depends on understanding deeply personal faith practices.

transcript.speaker: Alex Hormozi
transcript.message: And we need content partnerships established early. Religious publishers, seminary partnerships, pastoral endorsements - the credibility of our content will make or break adoption. I want to start building those relationships immediately while we're developing the core product.

transcript.speaker: Dave Ramsey
transcript.message: All valid, but we need financial projections. If we're doing content licensing, premium subscriptions, and potentially API licensing for our export format, what are the revenue targets? We need pricing models and customer acquisition costs mapped out before we build too much.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Excellent. We're converging on a clear scope. Priority one is the technical architecture extending CVS-003. Priority two is ethical frameworks for religious AI safety. Priority three is user research across faith traditions to validate product-market fit. Let me assign these actions and we'll reconvene mid-week with progress on all three fronts.

actionItems.text: Extend CVS-003 technical architecture to include Faith Journey spiritual AI guide builder
Design ethical frameworks and safeguards for religious AI accuracy
Conduct user research across major faith traditions for spiritual AI needs
Establish content partnerships with religious publishers and authorities
Develop pricing strategy and financial projections for Faith Journey monetization

topPriorities: Technical architecture for spiritual AI guide builder with export format
Ethical frameworks and safeguards for religious AI accuracy and safety
User research across major faith traditions to validate product-market fit

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
